storylines.moment: Day 6 of US strikes on Iran; IRGC hits two supertankers; Strait blockade fully operational
storylines.why_now: IRGC targeted two supertankers overnight in the Strait of Hormuz. The naval blockade reimposed Sunday at 4pm is holding. Trump reversed his ship-fee proposal within 24 hours of announcing it. Iran has formally declared the ceasefire void. Oil holding above $78/bbl; tanker insurers raising war-risk premiums.
storylines.hidden_question: Why does a strait 21 miles wide at its narrowest determine the price of oil in every country on earth?

history.note: Trump declares ceasefire 'over' at NATO summit; fresh US strikes launched after Iran targeted ships in Strait of Hormuz
US hits 90 targets across Iran's coastline; Iran retaliates against US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain
Pakistan and Qatar mediate; Trump says agreed to talks but ceasefire still 'over'; Strait of Hormuz remains the sticking point as shipping slows

Trump announces US will reinstate blockade of Strait, charge ships for passage. US Central Command: 140 Iran targets struck Sunday. Iran strikes US bases in Bahrain, Qatar, Kuwait, Oman, Jordan. Oil up 3.6% to $78.7/bbl.

IRGC hits two supertankers in Strait overnight. Trump reverses ship-fee proposal within 24 hours of announcing it. Naval blockade reimposed at 4pm Sunday holding. Iran formally declares ceasefire void. Tanker insurers raising war-risk premiums.

storylines.moment: Ukraine and Europe launch joint ballistic missile defense framework; Zelenskyy meets Macron in Paris on Bastille Day
storylines.why_now: Ukraine and European partners announced a joint ballistic missile defense framework today, direct response to Russia's July 6 mass attack (351 drones, 68 missiles, 22 dead in Kyiv). Zelenskyy traveled to Paris to meet Macron on Bastille Day, signaling the European defense axis is still cohering despite the Iran crisis dominating Western attention.
storylines.hidden_question: How did Russia build a secret fleet of hundreds of oil tankers that half the world quietly agreed to pretend not to see?

history.note: Russia launches mass attack: 351 drones, 68 missiles kill at least 22 in Kyiv — exposing gaps in Ukraine's air defenses
Ukraine shifts to offense: 36 shadow fleet tankers struck in 4 days; first-ever strike on Omsk refinery, 2,500km inside Russia; Crimea state of emergency declared
Ukraine and European partners announce joint ballistic missile defense framework. Zelenskyy meets Macron in Paris on Bastille Day.

storylines.moment: Ebola spreads to new regions of Congo — WHO tracking cases beyond Ituri epicenter
storylines.why_now: New suspected Ebola cases have appeared in parts of the Democratic Republic of Congo previously unaffected, signaling the outbreak is moving beyond the Ituri epicenter. WHO is monitoring; no international travel alert yet.
storylines.hidden_question: Why, with a highly effective Ebola vaccine available since 2020, does the same outbreak keep returning to the same region of Congo?

storylines.moment: Spain wildfire kills multiple in Almeria — victims found in burned-out vehicles
storylines.why_now: A fast-moving fire in the southern province of Almeria, a major holiday destination, trapped people in their cars as they tried to flee. Multiple fatalities confirmed. Coming weeks after Europe's early-summer heat surge.
storylines.hidden_question: Why do wildfires keep catching people in their cars even when they had time to evacuate?

storylines.moment: Senator Lindsey Graham dies at 71 — aortic dissection, day after returning from Ukraine
storylines.why_now: The four-term South Carolina Republican died Saturday evening of an aortic dissection after a brief and sudden illness. Graham had flown back from Kyiv the day before, where he'd been a prominent Ukraine supporter. His death opens a Senate seat in a reliably Republican state.
storylines.hidden_question: How did one of the most reliable opponents of democratic accountability at home become one of the Senate's most consistent champions of democracy abroad?

storylines.moment: Bangkok pub fire kills 27 — no fire escapes, exit doors reportedly locked
storylines.why_now: A fire broke out shortly before midnight Sunday at the Na Ladprao pub in Bangkok after smoke emerged from a circuit breaker near the stage. Most victims were found in windowless bathrooms near a rear exit. Firefighters brought the blaze under control in 30 minutes; 63 others hospitalized. Investigation underway.
storylines.hidden_question: Why do nightclub fires keep killing people the same way — locked exits, no sprinklers, crowds that can't find the way out — in every country, decade after decade?

storylines.moment: Heat dome expands east — Midwest hits today, Northeast tomorrow, 100°F+ possible
storylines.why_now: The heat system that set 148 daily records between June 30 and July 5 is now pushing into the Midwest and East Coast. Billings, MT could top 110°F for the first time in a century. Morning lows in cities staying in the mid-upper 70s — offering little overnight relief. Health alerts in effect across the region.
storylines.hidden_question: Why do we still measure heat waves by the daily high when it's the overnight low that actually kills people?

storylines.moment: DOJ opens investigation into Yale admissions — undergrad, law, and medical school
storylines.why_now: The Justice Department is investigating Yale's admissions practices across all three major divisions. Coming after Harvard and other elite institutions faced similar scrutiny, the investigation focuses on whether Yale's process complies with anti-discrimination law post the Supreme Court's 2023 affirmative action ruling.
storylines.hidden_question: After the Supreme Court banned race-conscious admissions, why do elite universities still end up with roughly the same kinds of students?

storylines.moment: State Dept vows to 'disable' ICC; sanctioned judges file lawsuit against US in Manhattan
storylines.why_now: The State Department escalated this week, adding ICC officials to the sanctions list and pledging to 'disable' the court's operations. In an unusual move, ICC judges filed a lawsuit in a Manhattan federal court challenging the sanctions' legality. The US is the only major democracy to sanction an international criminal court.
storylines.hidden_question: Why does the world's largest democracy impose sanctions on the court that was built to prosecute genocide?

storylines.moment: S&P downgrades Oracle to one notch above junk as AI spending hits $90B capex
storylines.why_now: S&P cut Oracle to BBB- on July 9 — the lowest investment grade — citing $167B in debt, $90–95B projected capex for FY2027, and the fact that OpenAI alone accounts for roughly half its remaining performance obligations. Oracle's stock rose anyway.
storylines.hidden_question: Why did Oracle's stock rise the same day S&P warned it was approaching junk — and what does that say about how investors are betting on AI?

storylines.moment: June CPI cools to 3.5% — but gas fell 9.7% during a ceasefire that no longer exists
storylines.why_now: CPI for June came in at 3.5% annually, beating expectations of 3.9%. Gas fell 9.7% in June — entirely due to the ceasefire that has since collapsed. Tech and semiconductor stocks bounced on the data Thursday and Friday. IBM's Q2 earnings today reversed that relief: shares plunged 24–25% on the worst single-day drop in the company's 115-year history.
storylines.hidden_question: What happens to inflation when the peace deal that lowered gas prices collapses?

history.note: Dow drops 1%+, Brent crude surges 5.7% on Trump's ceasefire announcement
Brent reverses to $76+ as conflict enters day two; Iranian retaliation against US bases confirmed
Oil 'relatively tame' as full-scale war doubts grow; Strait shipping slows; SK Hynix +13% debut adds bullish AI-chip counterweight
Brent crude jumps 3.6% to $78.7/bbl on Trump's Strait blockade announcement and 140-target US strike on Iran.

June CPI: 3.5% annual vs 3.9% expected; gas -9.7% in June (ceasefire effect, now gone). IBM crashes 24-25% on Q2 miss — worst day in 115-year history. Tech stocks that bounced on CPI data now retreating.

storylines.moment: IBM crashes 24% — worst day in 115-year history — as customers divert Q2 spend to AI hardware
storylines.why_now: IBM's Q2 earnings showed software and consulting revenue missed sharply. Executives said enterprise customers postponed IBM software contracts to redirect capex toward AI infrastructure — servers, memory, and storage to run their own AI. The selloff dragged ServiceNow -7%, Salesforce -5%, and Accenture -8%.
storylines.hidden_question: Why did IBM — which helped invent deep learning and supplies the mainframes that run the global financial system — keep ending up on the wrong side of the AI wave it helped start?

storylines.moment: SpaceX joins Nasdaq-100 after 15 trading days via rewritten index rules
storylines.why_now: Nasdaq rewrote its eligibility rules to add SpaceX — eliminating the float requirement and weighting the stock at 3× its actual float (roughly 1.3%). SpaceX qualified on market cap alone (top-40) just 15 trading days after its IPO. No other company has entered the Nasdaq-100 this quickly.
storylines.hidden_question: What does it mean when the rules of a major stock index get rewritten for a single company — and who benefits?

storylines.moment: Uber in advanced talks to acquire Delivery Hero at ~€10B — deal could close this week
storylines.why_now: Uber is reportedly in advanced negotiations to acquire Delivery Hero, the Berlin-based food delivery company that operates in Europe, Asia, and Latin America. At roughly €10B, the deal would make Uber the dominant food delivery platform across most of the developed world outside China.
storylines.hidden_question: What happens to food delivery gig work when one company owns everywhere?

storylines.moment: Mistral releases Leanstral 1.5 — AI that mathematically proves code is correct
storylines.why_now: Leanstral 1.5 uses the Lean 4 proof assistant to provide formal mathematical verification that software behaves as intended — moving beyond code generation into correctness guarantees. Relevant for aviation, medical devices, and financial systems where bugs are unacceptable.
storylines.hidden_question: Why have computer scientists been able to mathematically prove software is correct since the 1970s — and why does almost no production code use it?

storylines.moment: France vs Spain: today, Bastille Day in Dallas; England vs Argentina: tomorrow in Atlanta
storylines.why_now: France faces Spain today (July 14) at AT&T Stadium in Dallas — Bastille Day, with Mbappé on 8 goals and a chance to reach France's third consecutive World Cup final. Argentina players said this week they'd win tomorrow 'for the Malvinas.' The England-Argentina match in Atlanta is the first-ever World Cup semifinal between the two nations.
storylines.hidden_question: Why does every England vs. Argentina match feel less like a football game and more like unfinished business from a war?

history.note: Quarterfinals begin: France-Morocco and Spain-Belgium on July 10; Norway-England and Argentina-Switzerland on July 11
France 2-0 Morocco (Mbappé, Dembélé); Spain 2-1 Belgium (Merino 88'). Norway-England and Argentina-Switzerland play tonight
England beat Norway 2-1 AET; Argentina beat Switzerland 1-0 (Mac Allister 10'). All four QFs done. Semis: France vs Spain, England vs Argentina
Semis today and tomorrow. France vs Spain — Tue Jul 14, AT&T Stadium, Dallas, 3pm ET. England vs Argentina — Wed Jul 15, Mercedes-Benz Stadium, Atlanta, 3pm ET. Final July 19.

France plays Spain today (Bastille Day) in Dallas — Mbappé has 8 goals, France seeking third straight final. Argentine players said they'll win 'for the Malvinas.' First-ever WC semi between England and Argentina is tomorrow in Atlanta.

storylines.moment: Argentina reach semis; Egypt's FIFA protest over their match still unresolved
storylines.why_now: Argentina beat Switzerland 1-0 (Mac Allister 10') to reach the semifinals. Egypt's formal protest to FIFA over refereeing decisions in Argentina's 3-2 comeback win over Egypt remains open — no ruling as of July 12.
storylines.hidden_question: Has FIFA ever actually overturned a match result because of a refereeing protest — and if not, why do teams keep filing them?

history.note: Egypt leads Argentina 2-0 at halftime
Argentina completes stoppage-time comeback, wins 3-2
Egyptian FA officially protests refereeing decisions with FIFA
FIFA has not ruled on protest; Argentina faces Switzerland in QF tonight — protest shadow hangs over game
Argentina beat Switzerland 1-0 and advance to semis; Egypt's refereeing protest still unresolved by FIFA

storylines.moment: Live-action Moana opens — Dwayne Johnson reprises Maui a decade later
storylines.why_now: Disney's live-action remake of Moana debuted this weekend, with Dwayne Johnson reprising the role of Maui he voiced in the 2016 animated original. The film is tracking against a summer box office that has been dominated by sequels and remakes.
storylines.hidden_question: Why does Disney keep remaking animated films in live-action when the originals were made specifically because live-action couldn't do them justice?

storylines.moment: Sam Neill dies at 78 — 'sudden and unexpected,' had been cancer-free after blood cancer treatment
storylines.why_now: The Jurassic Park actor and star of dozens of films and the Peaky Blinders spinoff died this week. He had gone public about his blood cancer diagnosis in 2023 and written a memoir; he had been considered cancer-free. His death was described as sudden and unexpected.
storylines.hidden_question: Why do we only start paying attention to an actor's body of work after they die?
